headline: World Cup ICE Evidence Stops in March
deck: Washington embedded immigration agencies in the World Cup security stack before kickoff, yet public enforcement data ends in early March. Local officials report stadium restraint while advocates warn of detention risk and DHS weighs airport cuts. The record prices the gap until metro numbers publish.

art.title: Six Host Metros Under SEAR 1–2 Ratings
art.caption: Secret Service budget documents class World Cup matches as SEAR 1 and 2 high-risk special events from Seattle to New York, with CBP, TSA and CISA woven into the tournament’s federal coordination framework · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: A March 2025 presidential order created the White House Task Force on the FIFA World Cup 2026, chaired by the President with the DHS Secretary, Attorney General and FBI Director as members and housed administratively in DHS [E1]. The task force’s own page describes an Interagency Coordination Framework spanning CBP, TSA, CISA and DHS campaign materials, placing border and immigration agencies inside the tournament’s federal support stack [E2]. Secret Service budget documents class World Cup matches as SEAR 1 and SEAR 2 high-risk special events across multiple cities [E3]. Three readings compete for the enforcement picture: a surge under global scrutiny, a tournament pause, or baseline operations continuing beneath the spotlight [E1][E2][E3]. The documented architecture supplies footprints, not proof of coordinated intent in either direction [E1][E2].

Last summer’s Club World Cup set a precedent when CBP announced it would be “suited and booted” at the gates, deleted the post after alarm, and then called its presence at major sporting events routine [E10]. Each stage carries its own attribution: the initial post came from CBP, the deletion followed public alarm, and the routine characterization came in reporting that documented the sequence [E10]. A surge hypothesis would expect visible immigration branding at venue perimeters; the episode shows agencies present but reluctant to advertise enforcement optics at the turnstile [E10]. That pattern fits embedded security, with operations still possible away from cameras [E10]. Baseline operations at mega-events, by CBP’s later account, are the default posture when federal agencies fold into sports security [E10].

Los Angeles County Sheriff Robert Luna said federal officials told him no civil immigration enforcement by ICE was expected at World Cup matches in Los Angeles, while cautioning that posture could change [E4]. The assurance rests on the sheriff’s account of what federal officials conveyed, not on a published ICE pledge available as an independent primary release [E4]. New York Mayor Mamdani launched a “World Cup Referee Kit” blitz distributing red and yellow rights cards for ICE encounters, worker protections and hotlines for New Yorkers and visitors regardless of status [E5]. A pause hypothesis would read those local signals as coordinated restraint; they remain revocable assurances and municipal counter-programming without a published national stand-down order [E4][E5]. Metro leaders are preparing fans for encounters that federal messaging has not formally ruled out [E4][E5].

DHS drew up plans to halt immigration and customs processing at sanctuary-city airports, with Newark among those named, in a mid-tournament window according to May reporting [E12]. That pressure runs opposite the stadium assurances and would reach fans through arrival gates [E12]. The ACLU’s tournament campaign titled “know your rights, know your risks” characterizes host areas as under what the organization calls a militarized immigration crackdown, an advocacy characterization in the campaign’s own framing [E6]. More than 120 rights organizations issued a US travel advisory ahead of the World Cup warning visiting fans of detention risk [E7]. A surge hypothesis gains fuel from those warnings and the reported airport-processing threat, even without verified enforcement spikes at venues [E6][E7][E12].

Turnout through 44 matches defied the advisory climate: more than 2.85 million attendees filled stadiums to about 99.6 per cent of capacity [E8]. The figure cuts against an aggregate boycott driven by immigration fear, even as the advisory coalition numbered more than 120 organizations [E7][E8]. Fear reads as real and localized in the rights campaigns and travel warnings, while the aggregate spectator record shows the tournament absorbing that anxiety without empty seats [E6][E7][E8]. Neither full houses nor advisories alone adjudicate whether ICE operations surged, paused, or held steady away from turnstile optics [E8]. Capacity figures measure ticket demand, not encounter risk in transit corridors or processing queues [E8][E9].

State visitors still enter through the host’s ordinary visa machinery, with a FIFA-negotiated fast lane alongside it [E9]. Ticket holders can move to the front of the visa-appointment line through the tournament’s PASS channel, though an appointment does not guarantee approval [E9]. That enclosure of access runs parallel to the enforcement question: the State Department built a tournament-specific queue without publishing paired enforcement exemptions [E9]. PASS documents admission logistics, not a security truce at borders or airports [E9]. Fans who clear PASS still cross the same federal inspection layers DHS oversees for every inbound visitor [E2][E9].

The decisive evidentiary limit is temporal: the Deportation Data Project’s latest public release of arrests, detainers and removals runs only through early March 2026, three months before the tournament began [E11]. Any surge would expect elevated metro enforcement counts in those series once June and July figures publish, likely around September; a pause would expect a visible dip across host metros during match windows [E11]. Baseline continuity would show patterns continuing without a tournament-shaped inflection, and that is the reading the pre-kickoff record best supports today [E11]. The gap itself is the finding: neither advocates’ warnings nor local assurances nor near-full stadiums substitute for month-by-month enforcement accounting [E4][E7][E8][E11]. All three hypotheses stay in view until the March cutoff clears and autumn metro releases arrive [E11].

Footprints on the record include the presidential order, SEAR ratings, sheriff-reported assurances, sanctuary-airport processing threats, rights advisories, PASS fast lanes and attendance above 99 per cent [E1][E3][E4][E5][E7][E8][E9][E12]. Coordinated intent in either direction cannot be inferred from that mosaic: agencies are embedded, messaging conflicts, and the public datasets stop before kickoff [E2][E10][E11]. Resolution waits on metro enforcement releases the research community expects in the autumn window [E11]. The baseline reading is strongest on available numbers while the proof deficit is itself documented, which is why the epistemic label reads inference [E11]. Autumn data will sort surge, pause and baseline without establishing motives the footprints do not carry [E11].

evidence_box.fragment: Task Force on the FIFA World Cup 2026
